1: Moses's first objection to his commission: 'What if they do not believe me?' anticipates the perennial problem of divine credentialing. Jesus also faced the demand for signs that authenticate divine mission (John 2:18; Matthew 12:38-39). But where Moses needed external signs to validate his word, Jesus himself is the Word: his signs authenticate not a message about God but his own person as the Son of God (John 20:30-31). The credentials question is answered definitively in the resurrection.
2: The staff in Moses's hand — a shepherd's tool — becomes the instrument of miraculous works. The unremarkable instrument of daily labor is taken up by divine commission and empowered. Jesus likewise works through the ordinary: water, bread, spit, clay. The twelve are sent with no special equipment (Matthew 10:9-10), yet empowered for extraordinary works. The staff also foreshadows the cross, the instrument of Roman execution repurposed as the instrument of universal salvation.
3: A type: the staff thrown down becomes a serpent, recalling the serpent of Eden and anticipating the bronze serpent of Num 21. Jesus cites the wilderness serpent-on-a-pole episode directly as a type of his own crucifixion: 'as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life' (John 3:14-15). The serpent that bites is lifted up on a pole so that all who look to it may live — the one who became sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21) is lifted up on the cross for the life of the world.
4: Moses seizes the serpent by the tail at YHWH's command, and it becomes a staff again — the reversal of death and chaos back into an instrument of life and order. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate reversal: the serpent's sting (death) is swallowed up in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54-57). The one who had the power of death is disarmed (Hebrews 2:14); the instrument of death becomes the staff of the Good Shepherd who leads his people to living water (Revelation 7:17).
5: The purpose of the signs is faith: 'that they may believe that YHWH, the God of their fathers, has appeared to you.' The signs create and strengthen faith in the covenant God's active presence. John's Gospel is structured around seven signs (semeia), each designed to produce or deepen faith in Jesus as the Son of God (John 20:31). The goal of Exodus signs and Gospel signs is the same: not amazement at power, but trust in the Person behind the power.
6: The hand-in-cloak sign: healthy flesh becomes leprous, then restored. Leprosy in the OT is the paradigmatic condition of uncleanness — exclusion from the community, from worship, from touch. Jesus's healing of lepers (Matthew 8:1-4; Luke 17:11-19) repeatedly crosses the boundary that leprosy enforced. The restored hand in this sign anticipates YHWH's power to reverse the uncleanness that separates humanity from his presence — a reversal completed in Christ who 'touched the leper' and cleansed him.
7: Flesh restored from leprous to healthy — the reversal complete. The cleansing of leprosy is explicitly Christological in the Gospel accounts: when Jesus cleanses ten lepers and one returns to give thanks, Jesus notes he is a Samaritan (Luke 17:16), foreshadowing the gospel's reach to the Gentiles. The priestly procedure for cleansing leprosy in Leviticus 14 (two birds, blood, water, hyssop, scarlet thread) is itself a complex type of death, resurrection, and cleansing that the NT applies to Christ.
8: Escalating signs for escalating unbelief — if the first does not convince, the second may. The sign-structure here mirrors John's Gospel, where signs are given to provoke faith but also reveal hardness of heart (John 12:37-40). Jesus performs greater and greater works, yet unbelief persists. The Exodus pattern teaches that miracles alone do not produce saving faith; only the Spirit's work through the word creates genuine trust (John 6:63-65).
9: The third sign: Nile water poured on dry ground becomes blood. This anticipates the first plague (Exodus 7:14-24) and ultimately points to the blood of Christ — the blood of the new covenant poured out for many (Matthew 26:28). Water-to-blood as a sign of divine judgment is later reversed in the sign of water-from-Jesus's-side at the cross (John 19:34), where the blood of judgment and the water of cleansing flow together from the pierced Christ.
10: Moses's second objection: eloquence deficiency. Paul deliberately echoes this pattern: 'I did not come to you with eloquence or human wisdom... I resolved to know nothing except Jesus Christ and him crucified... in weakness and fear and much trembling' (1 Corinthians 2:1-3). The inadequate speaker is the consistent type of God's commissioned agent; the power is of God, not the messenger. Jesus himself, the Word of God, was despised for the ordinariness of his Galilean speech (Matthew 26:73).
11: YHWH declares himself the maker of the human mouth, the giver of sight and hearing. Jesus fulfills this claim directly: he heals the deaf, gives sight to the blind, looses the tongues of the mute (Matthew 11:5, citing Isaiah 35:5-6 as messianic credentials). The one who made the mouth comes in flesh to be the Word; the one who gave sight heals blindness as a sign of his identity as the light of the world (John 9:5-7).
12: 'I will help you speak and will teach you what to say.' The promise of divinely given words for the divine messenger is later deepened in Jesus's promise to the disciples: 'Do not worry about what to say or how to say it... it will not be you speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you' (Matthew 10:19-20). The Spirit of Christ given at Pentecost is the fulfillment of the Mosaic promise — the Paraclete who brings to mind all that Jesus taught (John 14:26).
13: 'Please send someone else' — Moses's resistance to the commission. The Christological contrast is sharp: Jesus does not resist but embraces the commission, 'Not my will, but yours be done' (Luke 22:42). In Gethsemane, where Moses said 'send another,' Jesus said 'nevertheless, not as I will.' The author of Hebrews makes the obedience of the Son the theological center: 'I have come to do your will, O God' (Hebrews 10:7, quoting Psalms 40:8).
14: YHWH's anger at Moses's reluctance, then the provision of Aaron as his spokesman. Moses as prophet-deliverer, Aaron as priestly voice — the two roles that will be unified in Christ, who is both prophet and priest (Hebrews 3:1; Exodus 4:14). The division of roles in Moses/Aaron points to a coming mediator who requires no imperfect human proxy because he is himself the Word of God incarnate and the perfect High Priest.
15: 'I will help both of you speak and will teach you what to do' — the two-person mediatorial team receives shared divine instruction. In Christ, the division between prophetic word and priestly action is overcome: he both speaks the Father's word and performs the Father's work (John 14:10), requiring no Aaron to speak what he cannot. The unity of word and deed in Jesus is what makes him the perfect mediator (1 Timothy 2:5).
16: 'He will speak to the people for you, and it will be as if he were your mouth and as if you were God to him.' Moses functions as 'god' to Aaron — a type of divine-human mediation. Paul in 2 Corinthians 5:20 describes the apostolic role: 'We are ambassadors for Christ, God making his appeal through us' — humans speaking for God, just as Aaron spoke for Moses. But the antitype is greater: Jesus does not need a human Aaron because he himself is the Word made flesh, his own mouth.
17: The staff of God — the shepherd's staff consecrated as the instrument of divine power in the Exodus. Jesus as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11-14) comes with authority to perform mighty works not by an external instrument but by his own word and touch. The 'staff of God' becomes, in the NT, the cross — the instrument through which the divine power of resurrection and salvation is exercised.
18: Moses returns to Jethro and asks leave to return to Egypt to see if his people still live. The departure of the divine agent from the place of safety to enter the domain of the enemy is the structure of the incarnation: Jesus 'came down from heaven' (John 6:38), leaving the Father's presence to enter the domain of sin and death. Moses returns to Egypt; Jesus enters the world where the enemy holds people captive.
19: A direct type: 'All those who wanted to kill you are dead.' Matthew 2:20 quotes this verse almost verbatim — the angel's instruction to Joseph in Egypt is 'those who were trying to take the child's life are dead,' using language that unmistakably echoes Exodus 4:19. The parallel identifies Jesus as the new Moses: as Moses was preserved from Pharaoh's murderous intent and called out of Egypt when it was safe, Jesus is preserved from Herod and called out of Egypt (Matthew 2:15, citing Hosea 11:1).
20: Moses takes his wife and sons on a donkey for the return journey to Egypt. The donkey connects forward to Jesus's entry into Jerusalem on a donkey (Matthew 21:5, fulfilling Zechariah 9:9) — the gentle king who comes not on a warhorse but on a beast of burden, as the one who carries the burdens of his people. The return from the place of safety to the place of confrontation is the structure of both Moses's return to Egypt and Jesus's final journey to Jerusalem.
21: YHWH tells Moses he will harden Pharaoh's heart — 'chazaq' (strengthen/harden). The hardening of Pharaoh is the great OT case study in divine sovereignty and human responsibility. Paul in Romans 9:17-18 cites Pharaoh as the definitive example: 'For this purpose I raised you up, to display my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.' The exodus signs are not merely demonstrations of power but revelations of divine glory through the contrast of sovereign grace and hardened resistance.
22: A direct type and foundational declaration: 'Israel is my firstborn son.' This is the first explicit father-son language for the covenant people in the Torah, and it generates the typological chain: Israel as son-of-God → Jesus as the true Son of God. Matthew 2:15 cites Hosea 11:1 ('Out of Egypt I called my son,' itself citing this Exodus pattern) as fulfilled in Jesus's return from Egypt after Herod's death. Israel is the type; Jesus is the antitype — the true and obedient Son who succeeds where national Israel failed (Matthew 3:17; Exodus 4:1-11, where the temptations recapitulate the wilderness testing).
23: 'I told you: let my son go so he may serve me. But you refused; so I will kill your firstborn son.' The exchange pattern at the heart of the Exodus: Pharaoh withholds YHWH's firstborn (Israel), so YHWH will take Egypt's firstborn. This life-for-life, firstborn-for-firstborn logic reaches its culmination at the cross: God gives his only Son (John 3:16), the true Firstborn over all creation (Colossians 1:15), as a substitutionary offering. 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Romans 8:32) deliberately echoes the Genesis 22 pattern of the firstborn not spared.
24: The mysterious night attack: 'At a lodging place on the way, the LORD met Moses and was about to kill him.' The sudden divine threat against the covenant agent before his mission fulfillment is one of the OT's most enigmatic passages. Theologically, it foreshadows the pattern that the deliverer himself must pass through mortal jeopardy — must face death — before accomplishing the deliverance. Jesus in Gethsemane faced the cup of death (Matthew 26:38-39) and the weight of divine judgment before the cross that accomplished salvation.
25: Zipporah's action: she circumcises her son and touches Moses with the foreskin, declaring 'you are a bridegroom of blood to me.' Circumcision is the covenant sign of cutting — a shedding of blood that enacts entrance into the covenant. The 'bridegroom of blood' phrase anticipates Christ as the one who, through the shedding of his blood, becomes the husband of the church (Ephesians 5:25-27; Revelation 19:7). The covenant community is constituted by blood — in the OT, circumcision; in the NT, the blood of Christ that seals the new covenant (Hebrews 9:15-18).
26: 'So the LORD let him alone. At that time she said bridegroom of blood, referring to circumcision.' The atonement is accomplished through blood: the threat is averted, the agent released to continue the mission. The pattern — mortal threat, blood shed, danger averted, mission continues — is the structure of the passion and resurrection. Christ faces death, sheds his blood, and the debt is paid; he is 'released' into resurrection and continues his mediatorial mission at the Father's right hand (Hebrews 7:25).
27: Aaron meets Moses at the mountain of God and kisses him. The reunion of the two brothers at the holy mountain is a scene of covenant fellowship restored. The mountain of God (Horeb/Sinai) is the locus of divine encounter throughout Exodus. Jesus's Transfiguration on a high mountain (Matthew 17:1-8) represents the eschatological fulfillment of the mountain-of-God encounters: the divine glory is no longer mediated through thunder and cloud but fully revealed in the person of the Son.
28: Moses tells Aaron everything YHWH had said and all the signs he had commanded. The perfect transmission of the divine message — the mediator communicates fully and faithfully. Jesus declares in John 17:8: 'I have given them the words you gave me, and they accepted them.' The faithful transmission of the divine word is the central act of the prophetic and apostolic mission; Christ is the fullness of that transmission, for he is the Word that the Father speaks (John 1:1-2).
29: Moses and Aaron gather the elders of Israel — the covenant assembly is constituted around the word and the signs. The church as the new covenant assembly (ekklesia — called-out assembly) is gathered by Christ through the apostolic proclamation (Matthew 16:18; Ephesians 2:20). The elders gathered before the exodus correspond to the disciples gathered before the final exodus that Jesus accomplishes in Jerusalem (Luke 9:31).
30: Aaron performs the signs before the people. The signs serve the word — they do not replace it but authenticate it. Paul in Romans 15:18-19 describes the apostolic mission as 'word and deed... the power of signs and wonders, through the power of the Spirit,' following the pattern established in Exodus: God's word goes forth through authorized agents whose message is authenticated by accompanying signs (Mark 16:20; Hebrews 2:3-4).
31: The people believe and bow down in worship when they see the signs and hear the word. Faith followed by worship: the pattern of authentic covenant response. The crowd at the triumphal entry (Matthew 21:8-9) offers acclamation; the disciples worship at the resurrection (Matthew 28:17). The Exodus pattern — signs create faith, faith produces worship — is fulfilled in the church gathered around the word and the sacraments that enact the new exodus.
